Standard Name/Number: AI Governance Playbooks
Summary: Provides voluntary governance playbooks for healthcare organizations implementing AI, organized around eight responsible-AI governance domains: organizational AI policy, organizational structure, organizational resources, responsible AI lifecycle management, risk and impact assessments, responsible data management and use, third-party management, and education, training, and feedback. The playbooks provide examples, implementation guidance, tools, and resources intended to help organizations integrate baseline AI governance controls into existing processes.
Healthcare Implications: The playbooks are directly relevant to health systems, hospitals, community health centers, developers, and governance teams because they translate responsible health-AI principles into operational controls for AI adoption, lifecycle oversight, risk assessment, data management, vendor oversight, training, and feedback processes. They are voluntary and do not create legal obligations, but they may shape institutional governance expectations and support alignment with the Joint Commission’s forthcoming voluntary Responsible Use of AI in Healthcare certification.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government; Patients & Public

Issuing Body: American College of Radiology; Society for Imaging Informatics in Medicine
Standard Name/Number: ACR–SIIM Practice Parameter for Imaging Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Practice parameter establishing operational expectations for responsible use of artificial intelligence in imaging workflows. The parameter defines infrastructure, personnel roles, governance processes, documentation and inventory practices, security and compliance considerations, model selection, local acceptance testing, user training, monitoring, and management of non-FDA-regulated models for imaging AI.
Healthcare Implications: Provides imaging practices with a concrete governance framework for selecting, deploying, monitoring, and improving AI tools in radiology and medical imaging. It is directly relevant to hospitals, imaging centers, radiologists, technologists, medical physicists, informatics teams, developers, and administrators because it sets expectations for AI governance committees, tool inventories, pre-deployment testing, performance monitoring, drift/safety review, privacy/security controls, and continuous quality improvement.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Standard Name/Number: IEC PAS 63621:2026 – Artificial intelligence enabled medical devices - Data management
Summary: Publicly available specification establishing a framework for managing the data lifecycle for AI-enabled medical devices, covering planning, acquisition, development, de-identification, dataset composition, annotation, quality improvement, verification, analysis, provisioning, and decommissioning.
Healthcare Implications: Provides developers and manufacturers of AI-enabled medical devices with concrete data-governance expectations that can support safer validation, better traceability, stronger bias mitigation, and more consistent documentation across the device lifecycle. Health systems using these tools can use it as a reference point for procurement, oversight, and internal governance.

Standard Name/Number: Use-Case Best Practice Guides and Testing & Evaluation Frameworks for Health AI
Summary: Series of consensus-based Best Practice Guides and Testing & Evaluation (T&E) frameworks that apply CHAI’s Responsible AI principles to four concrete health AI use cases, defining recommended practices and evaluation methods for developers and implementers.
Healthcare Implications: The guides and T&E frameworks give health systems, payers, and vendors detailed, use-case-specific methods and metrics to evaluate safety, bias, transparency, and clinical usefulness of AI tools in common workflows like patient-facing chatbots, prior authorization, EHR information retrieval, and clinical decision support, helping organizations operationalize responsible AI governance beyond high-level principles.

Summary: Consensus voluntary standard establishing a healthcare-organization framework for responsible AI governance across the lifecycle of AI applications, products, and technologies, including leadership accountability, regulatory alignment, data governance, ethical implementation, deployment, monitoring, and continuous improvement.
Healthcare Implications: Provides health systems and other healthcare organizations with a structured governance framework for evaluating, procuring, deploying, and overseeing AI tools in operational settings. It can shape internal governance, vendor management, risk oversight, and implementation practices even without binding legal force.

Standard Name/Number: Building a Healthier Future: Designing AI for Health Equity
Summary: Comprehensive 75-page blueprint from the NAACP that calls for “equity-first” standards in the design and deployment of AI in healthcare, warning that unchecked algorithms used in diagnosis, treatment, and insurance decisions can deepen racial disparities and outlining recommendations such as bias audits, transparency reporting, data governance councils, and community-based partnerships.
Healthcare Implications: Frames health AI as a civil-rights and health-equity issue, offering practical expectations that hospitals, payers, vendors, and regulators can use to evaluate whether AI tools and data practices center Black communities and other marginalized groups, and encouraging organizations to embed equity impact assessments, community engagement, and bias mitigation into every stage of health AI development and deployment.

Health systems, payers, and regulators can use the standard as a reference point when evaluating vendor evidence for accuracy, data quality, demographic representativeness, explainability, model drift planning, and real-world performance in clinical or administrative workflows.

Highlights the need for robust evidence of safety and effectiveness, monitoring for bias and adverse outcomes, appropriate consent and explanation to patients, and clear allocation of responsibility among clinicians, providers, and AI developers.

Healthcare Implications: Health systems and clinicians should prioritize AI solutions that provide clear, clinically useful explanations and documented evidence of safety and efficacy. Procurement, validation, and monitoring processes should include explainability, independent evaluation where feasible, and clinician‑facing documentation to support informed use.

Standard Name/Number: Ethical Guidance for AI in the Professional Practice of Health Service Psychology
Summary: The APA Ethics Committee issued guidance on how health service psychologists should integrate artificial intelligence into practice, grounding recommendations in the APA Ethics Code. The document addresses transparency with clients, informed consent, competence, privacy and data security, bias mitigation, documentation, and maintaining human clinical judgment when using AI-enabled tools in assessment, treatment, supervision, and practice management.
Healthcare Implications: The guidance offers a nonbinding but influential framework for psychologists and other mental health providers who are beginning to use AI for documentation, decision support, and digital interventions. By emphasizing human oversight, protection of sensitive health data, and active monitoring of bias and model limitations, it helps shape safer deployment of AI in behavioral health care and can serve as a benchmark for clinic policies and vendor design choices.

Summary: The Health IT End-Users Alliance released a consensus statement outlining principles for the development, deployment, and oversight of AI in healthcare from the perspective of clinicians, health information professionals, and other front-line users. Healthcare Implications: It provides a voluntary governance benchmark for hospitals, clinics, and health IT teams that are procuring or implementing AI tools. By centering end-user experience and calling for risk-based oversight and continuous monitoring, the statement can inform internal AI governance committees, procurement criteria, and vendor requirements, supporting safer and more trustworthy AI adoption in clinical, administrative, and population-health workflows.

Healthcare Implications: Provides a structured reference for clinicians, health systems, researchers, and regulators when designing, evaluating, or procuring AI tools, helping them operationalize concepts like fairness, robustness, transparency, and usability and aligning local governance programs and validation protocols with a widely cited global benchmark for trustworthy health AI.

It calls for multidisciplinary governance, rigorous external validation, and bias assessment across the Computer Vision care continuum (imaging, risk prediction, triage, and therapeutics).
Healthcare Implications: Cardiology programs using AI (e.g., Computer Vision imaging triage, risk models) can map local governance and study designs to the statement: require dataset documentation, bias audits, prospective evaluation before scale‑up, and ongoing performance surveillance with human oversight and clear accountability.

Healthcare Implications: Nursing leadership can translate the statement into practice by defining nurse responsibilities when using AI tools (documentation aids, clinical decision support), ensuring transparency to patients, establishing escalation pathways for algorithmic errors, and embedding AI ethics into competency and training programs.

Standard Name/Number: IEEE 7003-2022 – Algorithmic Bias Considerations
Summary: Provides processes and methodologies to address bias in the creation and deployment of algorithmic systems, including dataset selection, validation criteria, documentation, and communication of application boundaries.
Healthcare Implications: Directly applicable to clinical algorithm development and deployment (subgroup analysis, drift monitoring, performance reporting). Supports governance requirements in health‑equity and civil‑rights policies (e.g., Office for Civil Rights §1557 clinical algorithms).
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias

Standard Name/Number: IEEE 7002-2022 – Data Privacy Process
Summary: Specifies a systems/software engineering process for privacy-by-design, including requirements elicitation, data minimization, consent, and lifecycle controls. Applies to products and services processing personal data, including AI systems.
Healthcare Implications: Useful for digital health apps, remote monitoring platforms, and clinical AI tools processing PHI/PII. Supports privacy impact assessments and engineering controls aligned to healthcare compliance expectations.
Keyword Tags: Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance

Issuing Body: National Institute of Standards and Technology
Standard Name/Number: NIST SP 1270 – Identifying and Managing Bias in AI
Summary: Provides a structured approach to identifying and managing bias in AI systems, spanning data, human, and systemic sources. Addresses terminology, measurement, and evaluation practices to mitigate harmful outcomes.
Healthcare Implications: Directly applicable to clinical algorithm development and validation (e.g., subgroup performance, fairness metrics). Helps health systems design bias controls and documentation for oversight bodies and quality governance.
Keyword Tags: Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems

Standard Name/Number: IEEE 7001-2021 – Transparency of Autonomous Systems
Summary: Defines requirements for achieving and documenting transparency in autonomous/intelligent systems, including traceability, explainability, and communication to stakeholders. Supports accountability and human oversight by clarifying what information should be available and when.
Healthcare Implications: Applicable to AI-enabled clinical tools (e.g., imaging triage, decision support) to structure explainability artifacts and user communications. Helps providers evaluate vendor claims and set expectations for documentation and post‑market updates.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors

Standard Name/Number: Ethics and Governance of Artificial Intelligence for Health
Summary: CHIME’s AI Principles articulate CIO‑led expectations for responsible AI in health systems – covering governance, accountability, transparency, privacy/security, workforce readiness, vendor management, and equity – aimed at safe, value‑driven deployment of AI.
Healthcare Implications: Useful to shape enterprise AI governance charters and procurement checklists: require model documentation from vendors, privacy and security controls, bias/impact assessments, change‑control for model updates, and workforce training plans.
Keyword Tags: Equity & Bias; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
